nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef environments and rubble zones in tropical and subtropical marine waters

imageCandidates.notes: Product image from Mosaic Macros listing; shows grape-cluster bubble form on plug

summary: Red Bubble is a small red alga found across tropical reefs in the Caribbean, Atlantic, and Indo-Pacific. Its clustered, fluid-filled bladders resemble a tiny bunch of grapes and tend to glow with fluorescent-orange spots under actinic lighting.
careNotes:
Red Bubble (Botryocladia skottsbergii) is generally suited to nano and small reef tanks, reaching a maximum size of about 1–3 cm. Standard reef water parameters are generally advised: salinity around SG 1.023–1.025, temperature 24–27°C, and pH 8.0–8.5. Medium lighting tends to suit this genus well, though it has been observed in lower-light conditions in the wild. Growth is considered slow compared to other Botryocladia species, which makes it well-suited for display without the need for aggressive pruning.

The fluid-filled bladders will burst and release spores if mechanically damaged, so handle the specimen carefully and avoid placing it with aggressive grazers such as tangs, rabbitfish, sea urchins, or sea hares. Hobbyist reports indicate that some Botryocladia species can become persistent in tanks if bubbles rupture repeatedly; it is generally recommended to keep the specimen contained and remove any dislodged fragments promptly. Nutrient export capacity is considered low given the small size and slow growth. No allelopathy has been documented.

This species is sold as a display specimen attached to a plug and is not widely carried by most aquarium retailers — it is primarily available through specialty macroalgae vendors. Some older hobbyist references describe Botryocladia as unsuitable for home aquaria due to its invasive potential in unmanaged systems; this concern relates to unintentional infestations rather than intentional display culture on a plug in a well-managed reef.

As a red alga, this species will MELT at the base if buried in sand. Attach it to live rock using superglue, zip-tie, fishing line, or by wedging it into a crevice. Sand-bed planting is best reserved for genera such as Caulerpa, Halimeda, Penicillus, Rhipocephalus, and Udotea, along with some calcified Dasycladales.
breedingNotes: In the wild, Red Bubble propagates by releasing spores when its bladders rupture. In the aquarium, the documented method is fragmentation — carefully separating a piece of healthy thallus tissue (the algal body) and attaching it to a new surface. Spore release from ruptured bladders can also lead to secondary growth in the tank if conditions are favorable, which is why prompt removal of burst or dislodged fragments is generally advised.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source for this species. Confirms grape-cluster bubble morphology, actinic fluorescence, and small size (~1" max). Sold as display specimen on plug.

additional.name: WoRMS (World Register of Marine Species)
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy: Family Rhodymeniaceae, Order Rhodymeniales, Class Florideophyceae, Phylum Rhodophyta. Original name Chrysymenia skottsbergii Børgesen, 1924.

additional.name: Reeflex.net
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size (1–3 cm), temperature max (27°C), distribution (Indo-Pacific and Atlantic). Notes invasive potential in pest-infestation context. Water parameters: temp up to 27°C, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12.

additional.name: Reeflings (Botryocladia botryoides — genus cross-reference)
additional.notes: Genus-level husbandry cross-reference for Botryocladia. Confirms temperature 24–28°C, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.0–8.5, medium lighting (50–150 PAR), fragmentation propagation, and reef-safe compatibility. B. botryoides grows faster; B. skottsbergii is smaller and slower per Mosaic.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
